summary: This is a very brief post consisting of a single self-quote from Joel's longer article 'Good Software Takes Ten Years. Get Used To It.' The quote criticizes the software industry practice of adding and removing features as a way to justify new paid versions, using Microsoft's Clippy as the canonical example of a pointless feature addition.
key_points: Software companies struggle to justify new versions when they run out of meaningful features to add
The cycle of adding then removing unwanted features (like Clippy) is a cynical revenue strategy
Customers see through the tactic of charging for both additions and removals of features
money_quotes: Now the trouble comes when you can't think of any new features, so you put in the paperclip, and then you take out the paperclip, and you try to charge people both times, and they aren't falling for it.
key_insight: When software companies run out of genuine improvements, they resort to churning features in and out to justify new paid releases, and users recognize the ploy.
